introduction.p: Chapter 1 began with locations and displacements. It ended with distances to planes and hyperplanes. The main change was not a new formula; it was a new way to see geometry. Points tell us where. Vectors tell us how to move. Spans tell us what directions can build. Norms and distances tell us how large those motions are.
The problems below are meant to make those ideas usable. Some are short checks. Some are small discoveries. Draw pictures whenever a picture is possible.

exercises.title: Concept check: vectors as objects, not just coordinates

statement.p: What is the difference between a point and a vector, even when both are written with ordered pairs such as (3,4)?
Explain why the displacement from P=(2,-1,5) to Q=(7,3,1) is not the point Q, but the vector Q-P.
The same vector can be drawn with its tail at many different points. Why does moving the drawing of an arrow not change the vector it represents?
What does it mean for a vector to be a linear combination of two other vectors?
Why do the vectors (2,1),\qquad (4,2) not give two genuine directions?
Give an example of two vectors in \mathbb R^2 that are linearly independent. Explain how you know.
Give an example of three vectors in \mathbb R^3 that are linearly dependent even though none of them is the zero vector.
What two conditions must a list of vectors satisfy to be a basis?
Why can the zero vector not be normalized?
What is the difference between the sphere (x-1)^2+(y+2)^2=9 and the ball (x-1)^2+(y+2)^2\leq 9?
A plane can be described parametrically or by a normal equation. What does each description make easy to do?
Why is the equation 0x+0y+0z=4 not the equation of a plane?

exercises.title: Skills: vectors, distances, lines, and planes

statement.p: Let \mathbf u=(3,-1,4),\qquad \mathbf v=(-2,5,0). Compute \mathbf u+\mathbf v,\qquad 2\mathbf u-3\mathbf v,\qquad -\mathbf u.
Find the norm of each vector: (5,12),\qquad (2,-1,2),\qquad (1,1,1,1).
Find the distance between P=(1,2,3) and Q=(5,-1,9).
Find the equation of the sphere centered at C=(2,-1,4) with radius 6.
Find the center and radius of the sphere (x-3)^2+y^2+(z+5)^2=49.
Normalize the vector \mathbf v=(4,0,-3).
Write a parametric equation of the line through P=(1,0,2) in the direction \mathbf u=(3,-1,4).
Does the point (7,-2,10) lie on the line (x,y,z)=(1,0,2)+t(3,-1,4)?
Write a parametric equation of the plane through P=(0,1,2) in the directions \mathbf u=(1,0,3),\qquad \mathbf v=(2,1,0).
Find three points on the plane (x,y,z)=(0,1,2)+s(1,0,3)+t(2,1,0).
Find a normal equation for the plane through P_0=(1,2,3) with normal vector \mathbf n=(2,-1,5).
Does the point (3,4,1) lie on the plane 2x-y+5z=9?
Parametrize the plane x+2y-z=4 by choosing y=s and z=t.
Find the distance from A=(1,2,3) to the plane 2x-y+2z=7.
Find the closest point on the line L=(0,0)+t(2,1) to the point A=(3,4). Then find the distance from A to L.

exercises.title: Discovery: why two independent directions make a plane

introduction.p: These problems are meant to be explored slowly. They explain why linear independence is not just a definition, but the hidden rule behind coordinates.

statement.p: Let \mathbf u=(2,1),\qquad \mathbf v=(1,3). Try to solve s\mathbf u+t\mathbf v=(x,y) for s and t. Your answer should express s and t in terms of x and y. What does this prove about the span of \mathbf u and \mathbf v?
Repeat the previous problem with \mathbf u=(2,1),\qquad \mathbf v=(4,2). Which points (x,y) can be reached? Which cannot?

In \mathbb R^3, let \mathbf u=(1,0,1),\qquad \mathbf v=(0,1,1). Points in the span of \mathbf u and \mathbf v have the form s(1,0,1)+t(0,1,1). Write the coordinates of such a point as (x,y,z). Then find one equation involving x,y,z that every such point satisfies.
The previous problem found a plane through the origin. Translate it through the point P=(2,0,1). Write both a parametric equation and a normal equation for the translated plane.
Suppose three vectors in \mathbb R^3 all lie in the same plane through the origin. Explain why they must be linearly dependent.
Suppose three vectors in \mathbb R^3 are linearly independent. Explain why they cannot all lie in one plane through the origin.

A basis in \mathbb R^2 is like a pair of street directions. Use the basis \mathbf b_1=(1,1),\qquad \mathbf b_2=(1,-1) to express each vector below in \mathcal B-coordinates: (4,0),\qquad (0,6),\qquad (5,1).
Draw the grid formed by all points s(1,1)+t(1,-1) where s and t are integers between -3 and 3. How does this grid differ from the usual square coordinate grid?

exercises.title: Hook: the need for a multiplication of vectors

introduction.p: Addition and scaling have carried us far. But many geometric questions do not ask for another vector of the same kind.

statement.p: A force \mathbf F=(10,0) pushes an object through the displacement \mathbf d=(3,0). It seems natural that the work should be 30. What should the work be if \mathbf d=(0,3)? What about \mathbf d=(3,4)? What kind of vector multiplication would answer this?

The vectors \mathbf u=(3,0),\qquad \mathbf v=(0,2) form a rectangle of area 6. The vectors \mathbf a=(3,1),\qquad \mathbf b=(1,2) form a slanted parallelogram. Estimate its area from a drawing. Then try to find an algebraic rule that gives the signed area from the four components.
Swapping the order of two directions should not change the size of the parallelogram, but it should reverse its orientation. What should this suggest about the sign of an area product?

A multiplication that measures agreement in direction should be symmetric: \mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf u. A multiplication that measures oriented area should change sign when the order is swapped. Why might one product not be enough?

conclusion.p: The next chapter begins with this surprise: vectors have more than one useful kind of multiplication. One product measures how much two directions line up. Another measures the oriented area or volume they create. Those products will eventually lead to the dx\wedge dy that appears in differential forms, where signed area becomes an integrand rather than just a number.
